text: This video presents a robotic rejection of a central liver solitary fibrous tumor. This is the case of a thirty-year-old patient without medical history. The patient presented upper abdominal pain. A CT scan and MRI were performed and revealed an eight-centimeter tumor in the left liver, invading segment I and medial hepatic vein. A pathologic exam revealed a solitary fibrous tumor, and a robotic left hepatectomy extended to caudate lobe and median hepatic vein was planned.
On preoperative MRI with gadolinam injection in T-I sequence, we can see an heterogeneous hyper-oscular tumor located above the glycineal division, and evading Sandman-I and median hepatic vein.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart, while the surgical robot is docked from the head. Four robotic ports are utilized: one in the supra-umbilical position, two in the left hypochondrium, and one in the right hypochondrium.
The robotic arm lowers the hepatic dome to provide stable exposure of the supra-hepatic veins, while the surgeon performs dissection around the command trunk of the supra-hepatic vein.

The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy, sectioning the cystic artery and duct between hemlock clips. The hepatic pedicle is dissected with the assistance of the robotic arm for exposure. Hepatic arterial branches, including the left arterial branch and the branch for segment four, are identified and sectioned between hemlock clips to ensure hemostasis.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the left portal branch, securing it with hemlock clips before sectioning the vessel.
The surgeon releases the inferior vena cava and sections the right part of the caudate lobe, using a fold to aid in exposure.

The surgeon sections accessory infra-hepatic veins using hemlock clips. A monopolar coagulation device marks the parenchymal transection line in the anterior sector of the right side of the medial hepatic vein, following ultrasonographic anatomic analysis.

The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using a robotic hook, bipolar forceps, ultrasonic dissector, and vacuum irrigator. The irrigation enhances the efficiency of the bipolar energy, preventing coagulated tissue from adhering to the instruments and ensuring clear visibility of the forceps' tips.

The surgeon identifies a segment V vein draining into the median hepatic vein and sections it between hemlock clips. Tumoral resection necessitates extending the parenchymal resection in the anterior sector.

The left hepatic duct is identified and dissected. Indocyanine green injection is used to facilitate the identification of biliary convergence. The left hepatic duct is controlled with hematoclips after apparent chemo-liver transection to prevent injury to the right biliary structures.

The surgeon removes the tumor from the upper part of the portal division and dissects the caudate lobe. The anterior part of the inferior vena cava is exposed using an arm form, while the inferior vena cava is released.

The surgeon uses robotic bipolar forceps and a hook to control the dissection and manage accessory suprahepatic veins. Two bipolar forceps are employed to enhance exposure and achieve hemostasis along the transection line.

The surgeon identifies the venous branch from segment VIII draining into the median hepatic vein and sections it between hemlock clips. Upon completing the parenchymal transection, the inferior vena cava becomes visible.
The surgeon dissects segment four and the superior part of segment one from the inferior vena cava. The left part of the retroclobal ligament is controlled, coagulated, and sectioned.

The surgeon controls the median hepatic vein, using a loop to facilitate exposure, and sections it between hematoclips. The left hepatic vein is then controlled, exposed, and sectioned using a vascular stapler. The left triangular ligament is sectioned, and the specimen is removed through a subcostal incision.
The transection line is visible at the end of the operation, indicating the completion of the surgical procedure.

During a post-operative course, patient developed biliary fistula, dried up at post-operative day twenty, but it was discharged on post-operative day eight. Pathological exam confirmed solitary fibrous tumor, restricted completely. Patient was free from disease after three months.
